# Message in a Bottle

![Illustration of the surface of Europa with Jupiter and the Europa Clipper in the night sky. A rolled up scroll in a bottle that has "Your Name Here" on it is centered in the illustration.](https://assets.science.nasa.gov/dynamicimage/assets/science/cds/general/images/2017/12/europa-clipper-messag-ein-a-bottle.jpg?w=1200&h=800&fit=clip&crop=faces%2Cfocalpoint)

## Flying Your Name to Jupiter on NASA's Icy Moon Explorer

More than 2.6 million people around the world joined NASA’s Message in a Bottle campaign, a first-of-its-kind initiative that invited people to sign their names to a special message traveling 1.8 billion miles on the agency’s Europa Clipper spacecraft.

Europa Clipper launched on Oct. 14, 2024, on a mission to explore Jupiter’s ocean moon Europa, which is considered one of the most promising currently habitable environments in our solar system.

The message, a poem written by U.S. Poet Laureate Ada Limón, is engraved on the robotic spacecraft**.** Participant names will journey alongside the poem.

The campaign enhanced public involvement in NASA science and showcased the role of art in exploration—and sparked the imagination of people around the world.

## Campaign Overview

It’s not every day that members of the public have the chance to send their names into deep space beyond Mars, all the way to Jupiter and its moon Europa. But with NASA’s Europa Clipper, NASA gave the public that opportunity: Names are etched on a microchip affixed to the spacecraft that is traveling 1.8 billion miles (2.6 billion kilometers) to Europa, where an ocean hides beneath a frozen outer shell.

## More than 2.6 million people around the world joined NASA's Message in a Bottle campaign — setting a new record for the most names ever flown on a spacecraft beyond Mars.

![](https://assets.science.nasa.gov/dynamicimage/assets/science/cds/general/images/2024/05/europa-clipper-19c754.jpg?w=3000&h=2000&fit=clip&crop=faces%2Cfocalpoint)

## 

### 

Hannah is an aspiring astronaut, an artist, a poet — and the 1 millionth Earthling to sign up for NASA's Message in a Bottle campaign. The campaign sparked the imagination of people around the world.

![A young girl holding up a handwritten message to Europa.](https://assets.science.nasa.gov/dynamicimage/assets/science/missions/europa-clipper/images/hannah_message_in_a_bottle.jpg?w=950&h=1424&fit=clip&crop=faces%2Cfocalpoint)

The chip is attached to a metal plate engraved with the original poem “In Praise of Mystery: A Poem for Europa,” written by U.S. Poet Laureate Ada Limón to celebrate the mission. Riding on the exterior of the spacecraft, the poem and names will be like a message in a bottle as they make about 50 close to flybys of the ocean world. The mission will gather data to determine if Europa could support life.

![](https://assets.science.nasa.gov/dynamicimage/assets/science/missions/europa-clipper/images/Your%20Name%20is%20Here.jpg?w=8272&h=6200&fit=clip&crop=faces%2Cfocalpoint)

The campaign inspired people around the world. Between June and December 2023, NASA collected over 2.6 million names to send to the Jupiter system. By coming aboard, participants get the chance to be part of history as NASA looks to answer some of humanity’s biggest questions: How does the universe work, and are we alone?

## NASA Partners with U.S. Poet Laureate Ada Limón

![Foto de Ada Lim�n observando la sala limpia en donde est� siendo ensamblada la nave espacial Europa Clipper de la NASA.](https://assets.science.nasa.gov/dynamicimage/assets/science/cds/ciencia/2023/12/ada-limon-jpl.png?w=1982&h=1322&fit=clip&crop=faces%2Cfocalpoint)

The campaign is a [first-of-its-kind collaboration](https://europa.nasa.gov/news/91/nasa-invites-public-to-sign-poem-that-will-fly-aboard-europa-clipper/), uniting art and science, by NASA, the U.S. Poet Laureate, and the Library of Congress. Participants aren’t just having their names flown on a spacecraft—they’re signing their names to a poem written by U.S. Poet Laureate Ada Limón. The poem connects the two water worlds — Earth, yearning to reach out and understand what makes a world habitable, and Europa, waiting with secrets yet to be explored.

The poem was revealed at a [public event](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6oN37cmGU-k) hosted at the Library of Congress on June 1, 2023. The event was steamed live by the Library of Congress, on NASA TV, and carried on PBS and space.com.

## A Legacy of Inspiration

The campaign is similar to other NASA projects that have enabled tens of millions of people to send their names to ride along with Artemis I and several Mars spacecraft. It draws from the agency’s long tradition of shipping inspirational messages on spacecraft that have explored our solar system and beyond. In the vein of [NASA’s Voyagers’ Golden Record](https://voyager.jpl.nasa.gov/golden-record/), which sent a time capsule of sounds and images to communicate the diversity of life and culture on Earth, the program aims to spark the imagination of people around the world.

## How Names Are Etched on the Microchip

Technicians in the [Microdevices Laboratory at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory](https://www.jpl.nasa.gov/virtual-tour/) in Southern California used an electron beam to stencil participants’ names onto a thumbnail-sized silicon microchip. Each line of text is smaller than 1/1000th the width of a human hair (75 nanometers). The public engagement team produced a behind-the-scenes tour of the lab where the microchip is produced, showing participants how it works.

## Innovative Website

While the campaign to gather names was active, a dedicated microsite allowed users to dive through Europa’s ice, read the poem by U.S. Poet Laureate Ada Limón, and sign up for the campaign. Upon signing up, users were presented with a social media shareable graphic customized with their name. Users could download, print, or share the graphic on their social networks, using native share trays on mobile devices.

![Blue website banner with graphics inviting readers to send their names to Europa.](https://assets.science.nasa.gov/dynamicimage/assets/science/missions/europa-clipper/images/europa_clipper_sign_on_banner.jpg?w=2000&h=1145&fit=clip&crop=faces%2Cfocalpoint)
![A blue page with a poem on the left side in a yellow-colored box. There also is a place to sign up to send your name to Europa.](https://assets.science.nasa.gov/dynamicimage/assets/science/missions/europa-clipper/images/message_in_a_bottle_sign_on_page_with_poem.jpg?w=2000&h=1177&fit=clip&crop=faces%2Cfocalpoint)
![Two images showing what readers saw when they signed up for the Message in a Bottle Campaign.](https://assets.science.nasa.gov/dynamicimage/assets/science/missions/europa-clipper/images/message_in_a_bottle_share_signups.jpg?w=2500&h=1421&fit=clip&crop=faces%2Cfocalpoint)

## Global Participation

Space exploration transcends borders. Our global outreach efforts resulted in participation around the world. Coupled with our media and social outreach, we worked with partner organizations to help spread the word, including NASA’s Solar System Ambassadors and the [Girl Scouts of USA](https://www.instagram.com/p/C0mfSEau3NX/).

![A blue map showing where people lived who had signed up to send their name to Europa.](https://assets.science.nasa.gov/dynamicimage/assets/science/missions/europa-clipper/images/message_in_a_bottle_participation_map.jpg?w=2000&h=1066&fit=clip&crop=faces%2Cfocalpoint)

## Social Media

Our social media campaign ignited imaginations worldwide. We leveraged NASA’s full social media reach to raise awareness of the campaign on a global scale. Participants received [social shareables](https://x.com/NASAScienceAA/status/1728580667540168914?s=20), customized with their names, that they could show off to friends and family. We welcomed our [Sesame Street](https://x.com/sesamestreet/status/1678766100954370049?s=20) friends and [Snoopy](https://www.instagram.com/p/C0R_iJdPnev/?hl=en) on board, along [science communicators](https://www.instagram.com/reel/C0UENClPqao/?utm_source=ig_web_copy_link&igshid=MzRlODBiNWFlZA==) and [other spacecraft](https://x.com/NASAPersevere/status/1719078067854090316?s=20). Meanwhile, a [social countdown campaign](https://x.com/NASA/status/1733950166472786311?s=20) started several weeks from the deadline, using shareables to tell the public how much time they have left to sign on.

## Activating NASA’s Social Channels

The campaign was promoted by over 50 NASA social media accounts, linking to related NASA science and the agency's legacy of exploration. Select posts below show how the campaign was integrated across NASA missions and accounts.

![A blue, watery image background with two social media posts on top.](https://assets.science.nasa.gov/dynamicimage/assets/science/missions/europa-clipper/images/mssage_in_a_bottle_social_share_examples.jpg?w=2500&h=1421&fit=clip&crop=faces%2Cfocalpoint)
![A blue, watery image background with two social media posts on top.](https://assets.science.nasa.gov/dynamicimage/assets/science/missions/europa-clipper/images/message_in_a_bottle_social_media_share_2.jpg?w=2500&h=1421&fit=clip&crop=faces%2Cfocalpoint)

## High-Flying Friends

NASA partnered with [Sesame Street](https://x.com/sesamestreet/status/1678766100954370049?s=20) and [Snoopy](https://www.instagram.com/p/C0R_iJdPnev/?hl=en) to promote the campaign with three dedicated social collaborations. Sesame Street released custom artwork featuring Elmo holding a bottle in space, and characters engaged with various NASA accounts. In addition, Sesame Street dedicated the "Letter of the Day" to be "M for moon." Snoopy content included a clip from the Apple TV "Snoopy in Space" cartoon show, in which Snoopy goes to Europa. Singer and actor Lance Bass even stopped by NASA's Jet Propulsion Laboratory to promote the campaign.

![A blue, watery image background with two social media posts from Sesame Street.](https://assets.science.nasa.gov/dynamicimage/assets/science/missions/europa-clipper/images/message_in_a_bottle_sesame_street_share_example.jpg?w=2500&h=1421&fit=clip&crop=faces%2Cfocalpoint)

## Fellow Missions

NASA spacecraft including [Perseverance](https://twitter.com/NASAPersevere/status/1719078067854090316) and [Curiosity](https://x.com/MarsCuriosity/status/1733203541089509782?s=20) signed on, along with the European Space Agency's Juice spacecraft.

![A blue, watery image background with two social media posts on top.](https://assets.science.nasa.gov/dynamicimage/assets/science/missions/europa-clipper/images/fellow_missions_message_in_a_bottle.jpg?w=2500&h=1421&fit=clip&crop=faces%2Cfocalpoint)

## Content Creators

Content creators organically promoted the campaign across their channels. One [video, produced by Australian space and science communicator Kobi Brown](https://www.youtube.com/shorts/WhfbhCwYgYg), received more than 5 million views.

![A blue, watery image background with an image of a person, and two images of Europa Clipper.](https://assets.science.nasa.gov/dynamicimage/assets/science/missions/europa-clipper/images/message_in_a_bottle_content_creators.jpg?w=2500&h=1421&fit=clip&crop=faces%2Cfocalpoint)

## Twitch

The campaign was promoted as part of a [live stream on the NASA Twitch](https://www.twitch.tv/videos/2002247601) account, giving viewers a behind-the-scenes look at the Europa Clipper spacecraft while it was being assembled in a clean room at NASA's Jet Propulsion Laboratory.

![A large room with lots of equipment for building a spacecraft.](https://assets.science.nasa.gov/dynamicimage/assets/science/missions/europa-clipper/images/europa_clipper_live_cam.jpg?w=2540&h=1414&fit=clip&crop=faces%2Cfocalpoint)

## Spanish

We created a dedicated Spanish microsite for the campaign. Translating poetry is tricky, but we teamed up with Puerto Rican poet Roque Raquel Salas Rivera to bring Ada Limón's poem to life in Spanish. The campaign was promoted across [NASA en español](https://x.com/NASA_es/status/1730602654726803671?s=20) social media accounts on [NASA Ciencia](https://ciencia.nasa.gov/).

![Blue website banner in Spanish with graphics inviting readers to send their names to Europa.](https://assets.science.nasa.gov/dynamicimage/assets/science/missions/europa-clipper/images/message_in_a_bottle_spanish_site.jpg?w=2500&h=1440&fit=clip&crop=faces%2Cfocalpoint)

This mission has made hundreds of headlines in more than 10 languages worldwide. From [AP News](https://apnews.com/article/ada-limon-poet-laureate-nasa-f7933c037f9ae719c8209bdedebd28cf) to [NPR](https://www.npr.org/2023/12/11/1218489539/nasa-is-encouraging-people-to-submit-their-names-to-be-sent-into-deep-space), [space.com](https://www.space.com/nasa-clipper-moon-jupiter-europa-human-names-microchip) to [Smithsonian Magazine](https://www.smithsonianmag.com/smart-news/read-the-poem-nasa-will-send-to-jupiters-moon-europa-180982312/), the [Literary Hub](https://lithub.com/nasa-is-sending-an-ada-limon-poem-into-space/) to the [Washington Post](https://www.washingtonpost.com/science/2023/11/19/nasa-name-in-space-europa/), everyone has been talking about this journey to Jupiter. We even got meteorologists involved, encouraging people to gaze up at Jupiter and join our mission.

![A blue, watery image background with samples of media posts and an image of Ada Limon.](https://assets.science.nasa.gov/dynamicimage/assets/science/missions/europa-clipper/images/message_in_a_bottle_media_coverage.jpg?w=2500&h=1421&fit=clip&crop=faces%2Cfocalpoint)

## Revealing the Vault Plate

During the [SXSW 2024 Opening Session](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yLTzAwe6i9c), NASA's Planetary Science Division Director Dr. Lori Glaze and U.S. Poet Laureate Ada Limón revealed the design for the commemorative vault plate that carries participant names. At the heart of the triangular metal plate is the engraving of U.S. Poet Laureate Ada Limón’s handwritten “In Praise of Mystery: A Poem for Europa,” along with the silicon microchip stenciled with more than 2.6 million names submitted by the public. The microchip is the centerpiece of an illustration of a bottle amid the Jovian system — a reference to NASA’s “Message in a Bottle” campaign, which invited the public to send their names with the spacecraft.

Made of the metal tantalum and about 7 by 11 inches (18 by 28 centimeters), the plate features graphic elements on both sides. The outward-facing panel features art that highlights Earth’s connection to Europa. Linguists collected recordings of the word “water” spoken in 103 languages, from families of languages around the world. The audio files were converted into waveforms (visual representations of sound waves) and etched into the plate. The waveforms radiate out from a symbol representing the American Sign Language sign for “water.”

# Europa Clipper

Europa Clipper launched Oct. 14, 2024, on a journey to explore Europa, Jupiter's ocean world.

Mission overview

why europa

your name is here!

![](https://science.nasa.gov/wp-content/uploads/2017/12/europa-clipper-liftoff-hi-res-1920x640-1.jpg?w=1536)

Europa Clipper is the first mission designed to conduct a detailed study of Jupiter's moon Europa. There’s scientific evidence that the ingredients for life may exist on Europa right now. The spacecraft will travel 1.8 billion miles (2.9 billion kilometers) to reach Jupiter in April 2030. It will orbit Jupiter, and conduct 49 close flybys of Europa.

Type

LaunchED

Target

Objective

## What Will Europa Clipper Do?

Europa Clipper will try to determine if Jupiter's icy moon Europa currently has habitable conditions.

Europa Clipper will travel 1.8 billion miles (2.9 billion km) to reach Jupiter in April 2030. The spacecraft will orbit Jupiter, and conduct 49 close flybys of Europa. The spacecraft carries nine science instruments, and a gravity experiment that uses the telecommunications system. All science instruments will operate simultaneously on every pass.

![Artist’s rendering of NASA’s Europa Clipper spacecraft.](https://science.nasa.gov/wp-content/uploads/2024/05/europa-clipper-19c754.jpg?w=1024)

## Science Goals

What is Europa Clipper's main science goal?

Europa Clipper’s main science goal is to determine whether there are places below the surface of Jupiter’s icy moon, Europa, that could support life.

![A side view of what Europa what look like if we could slice into it. There's beige, and white crust with reddish streaks and plumes erupting. Below the crust is a blue ocean on top of a brown crust with a reddish hotspot in the lower left corner.](https://assets.science.nasa.gov/dynamicimage/assets/science/cds/general/images/2024/09/europas-mysterious-interior-artists-concept.jpg?w=2550&h=2700&fit=clip&crop=faces%2Cfocalpoint)

## Meet the Europa Clipper Spacecraft

Europa Clipper is a robotic solar-powered spacecraft built to conduct the first detailed investigations of Jupiter's icy moon Europa.

With its solar arrays deployed, Europa Clipper spans more than 100 feet (about 30 meters) – about the length of a basketball court. The main body of the spacecraft consists of its avionics vault, radio frequency module, and propulsion module.

![The silver-colored Europa Clipper sits on a work stand in a clean room. Workers in protective clothing stand nearby.](https://assets.science.nasa.gov/dynamicimage/assets/science/cds/general/images/2024/05/europa-clipper-with-high-gain-antenna-pia25958.jpg?w=7959&h=5306&fit=clip&crop=faces%2Cfocalpoint)

## Why Go to Europa?

The search for life beyond Earth is one of NASA’s primary objectives.

If humans are to truly understand our place in the universe, we must learn whether our planet is the only place where life exists. So the search is on! There is strong evidence Jupiter's moon Europa has a saltwater ocean that may be one of the best places to look for environments where life could exist beyond Earth.

![The pale gray of Europa is seen against the blackness of space.](https://science.nasa.gov/wp-content/uploads/2008/03/europa-junocam-jpg.webp?w=914)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Europa_Clipper_patch.png/250px-Europa_Clipper_patch.png)

**Europa Clipper** (previously known as **Europa Multiple Flyby Mission**) is a [space probe](/wiki/Space_probe "Space probe") developed by [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA") to study [Europa](/wiki/Europa_(moon) "Europa (moon)"), a [Galilean moon](/wiki/Galilean_moon "Galilean moon") of [Jupiter](/wiki/Jupiter "Jupiter"). It was launched on October 14, 2024.[[15]](#cite_note-15) The spacecraft used a [gravity assist](/wiki/Gravity_assist "Gravity assist") from Mars on March 1, 2025,[[10]](#cite_note-auto2-10) and it will use a gravity assist from Earth on December 3, 2026,[[11]](#cite_note-auto1-11) before arriving at Europa in April 2030.[[16]](#cite_note-NASA_use_commercial-16) The spacecraft will then perform a series of [flybys](/wiki/Flyby_(spaceflight) "Flyby (spaceflight)") of Europa while orbiting Jupiter.[[17]](#cite_note-TG-20230305-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)

Europa Clipper is designed to study evidence for a subsurface ocean underneath Europa's ice [crust](/wiki/Crust_(geology) "Crust (geology)"), found by the [Galileo](/wiki/Galileo_(spacecraft) "Galileo (spacecraft)") spacecraft which orbited Jupiter from 1995 to 2003. Plans to send a spacecraft to Europa were conceived with projects such as [Europa Orbiter](/wiki/Europa_Orbiter "Europa Orbiter") and [Jupiter Icy Moons Orbiter](/wiki/Jupiter_Icy_Moons_Orbiter "Jupiter Icy Moons Orbiter"), in which a spacecraft would be inserted into orbit around Europa. However, due to the effects of [radiation](/wiki/Radiation "Radiation") from the [magnetosphere of Jupiter](/wiki/Magnetosphere_of_Jupiter "Magnetosphere of Jupiter") in Europa orbit, it was decided that it would be safer to insert a spacecraft into an [elliptical orbit](/wiki/Elliptic_orbit "Elliptic orbit") around Jupiter and make 49 close flybys of the moon instead.[[19]](#cite_note-19) The Europa Clipper spacecraft is larger than any previous spacecraft for NASA planetary missions.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

The orbiter will analyze the [induced magnetic field around Europa](/wiki/Europa_(moon)#Internal_structure "Europa (moon)"), and attempt to detect plumes of water [ejecta](/wiki/Ejecta "Ejecta") from a subsurface ocean; in addition to various other tests.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

The mission's name is a reference to the lightweight, fast [clipper ships](/wiki/Clipper "Clipper") of the 19th century that routinely plied trade routes, since the spacecraft will pass by Europa at a rapid cadence, as frequently as every two weeks. The mission patch, which depicts a sailing ship, references the moniker.[[22]](#cite_note-Clipper_name-22)

Europa Clipper complements the [ESA](/wiki/European_Space_Agency "European Space Agency")'s [Jupiter Icy Moons Explorer](/wiki/Jupiter_Icy_Moons_Explorer "Jupiter Icy Moons Explorer"), launched in 2023, which will attempt to fly past Europa twice and [Callisto](/wiki/Callisto_(moon) "Callisto (moon)") multiple times before moving into orbit around [Ganymede](/wiki/Ganymede_(moon) "Ganymede (moon)").

## History

### Early proposals and Galileo discoveries

In 1997, a [Europa Orbiter](/wiki/Europa_Orbiter "Europa Orbiter") mission was proposed by a team for NASA's [Discovery Program](/wiki/Discovery_Program "Discovery Program")[[23]](#cite_note-The_Europa_Ocean_Discovery_mission-23) but was not selected. NASA's JPL announced one month after the selection of Discovery proposals that a NASA Europa orbiter mission would be conducted. JPL then invited the Discovery proposal team to be the Mission Review Committee (MRC).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

At the same time as the proposal of the Discovery-class Europa Orbiter, the robotic [Galileo](/wiki/Galileo_(spacecraft) "Galileo (spacecraft)") spacecraft was already orbiting Jupiter. From December 8, 1995, to December 7, 1997, Galileo conducted the primary mission after entering the orbit of Jupiter. On that final date, the Galileo orbiter commenced an extended mission known as the Galileo Europa Mission (GEM), which ran until December 31, 1999. This was a low-cost mission extension with a budget of only US$30 million. The smaller team of about 40–50 people (compared with the primary mission's 200-person team from 1995 to 1997) did not have the resources to deal with problems, but when they arose, it was able to temporarily recall former team members (called "tiger teams") for intensive efforts to solve them. The spacecraft made several flybys of Europa (8), [Callisto](/wiki/Callisto_(moon) "Callisto (moon)") (4) and [Io](/wiki/Io_(moon) "Io (moon)") (2). On each flyby of the three moons it encountered, the spacecraft collected only two days' worth of data instead of the seven it had collected during the primary mission.[[24]](#cite_note-:0-24) During GEM's eight flybys of Europa, it ranged from 196 to 3,582 km (122 to 2,226 mi), in two years.[[24]](#cite_note-:0-24)

Europa has been identified as one of the locations in the [Solar System](/wiki/Solar_System "Solar System") that could possibly harbor microbial [extraterrestrial life](/wiki/Extraterrestrial_life "Extraterrestrial life").[[25]](#cite_note-Europa_a_must-25)[[26]](#cite_note-Schulze-Makuch2001-26)[[27]](#cite_note-zab-27) Immediately following the Galileo spacecraft's discoveries and the independent Discovery program proposal for a Europa orbiter, JPL conducted preliminary mission studies that envisioned a capable spacecraft such as the [Jupiter Icy Moons Orbiter](/wiki/Jupiter_Icy_Moons_Orbiter "Jupiter Icy Moons Orbiter") (a US$16 billion mission concept),[[28]](#cite_note-28) the [Jupiter Europa Orbiter](/wiki/Jupiter_Europa_Orbiter "Jupiter Europa Orbiter") (a US$4.3 billion concept), another orbiter (US$2 billion concept), and a multi-flyby spacecraft: Europa Clipper.[[29]](#cite_note-Kane_2014-29)

A mission to Europa was recommended by the [National Research Council](/wiki/National_Research_Council_(United_States) "National Research Council (United States)") in 2013.[[25]](#cite_note-Europa_a_must-25)[[27]](#cite_note-zab-27) The approximate cost estimate rose from US$2 billion in 2013 to US$4.25 billion in 2020.[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31) The mission is a joint project between the [Johns Hopkins University](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_University "Johns Hopkins University")'s [Applied Physics Laboratory](/wiki/Applied_Physics_Laboratory "Applied Physics Laboratory") (APL), and the [Jet Propulsion Laboratory](/wiki/Jet_Propulsion_Laboratory "Jet Propulsion Laboratory") (JPL).[[1]](#cite_note-Leone-1)[[32]](#cite_note-OPAG_Update_2013-32)

### Funding put forward

In March 2013, US$75 million was authorized to expand on the formulation of mission activities, mature the proposed science goals, and fund preliminary instrument development,[[33]](#cite_note-Europa_SETI-33) as suggested in 2011 by the [Planetary Science Decadal Survey](/wiki/Planetary_Science_Decadal_Survey "Planetary Science Decadal Survey").[[1]](#cite_note-Leone-1)[[27]](#cite_note-zab-27) In May 2014, a House bill substantially increased the Europa Clipper (referred to as *Europa Multiple Flyby Mission*) funding budget for the 2014 fiscal year from US$15 million[[34]](#cite_note-March_2014-34)[[35]](#cite_note-Clark_2014-35) to US$100 million to be applied to pre-formulation work.[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37) Following the [2014 election cycle](/wiki/United_States_elections,_2014 "United States elections, 2014"), bipartisan support was pledged to continue funding for the *Europa Multiple Flyby Mission* project.[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39) The executive branch also granted US$30 million for preliminary studies.[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-Clark2015-41)

### Formulation

In April 2015, NASA invited the [ESA](/wiki/European_Space_Agency "European Space Agency") to submit concepts for an additional probe to fly together with the Europa Clipper spacecraft, with a mass limit of 250 kg.[[42]](#cite_note-Benedetto_2016-42) It could be a simple probe, an impactor,[[43]](#cite_note-43) or a lander.[[44]](#cite_note-Clark_2015-44) An internal assessment at ESA considered whether there was interest and funds available,[[45]](#cite_note-45)[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[47]](#cite_note-researchIT-47)[[48]](#cite_note-JEM2017-48) opening a collaboration scheme similar to the very successful [Cassini–Huygens](/wiki/Cassini%E2%80%93Huygens "Cassini–Huygens") approach.[[48]](#cite_note-JEM2017-48)

In May 2015, NASA chose nine instruments that would fly on board the orbiter, budgeted to cost about US$110 million over the next three years.[[49]](#cite_note-49) In June 2015, NASA approved the mission concept, allowing the orbiter to move to its formulation stage.[[50]](#cite_note-50) In January 2016, NASA approved the addition of a lander,[[51]](#cite_note-2016_Funds-51)[[52]](#cite_note-Lander_funding-52) but this was canceled in 2017 because it was deemed too risky.[[53]](#cite_note-jpl-science-report-53) In May 2016, the [Ocean Worlds Exploration Program](/wiki/Ocean_Worlds_Exploration_Program "Ocean Worlds Exploration Program") was approved,[[54]](#cite_note-54) of which the Europa mission is part.[[55]](#cite_note-OWEP-55)

In February 2017, the mission moved from Phase A to Phase B (the preliminary design phase).[[56]](#cite_note-Greicius-56) On July 18, 2017, the House Space Subcommittee held hearings on the Europa Clipper as a scheduled [Large Strategic Science Missions](/wiki/Large_Strategic_Science_Missions "Large Strategic Science Missions") class, and to discuss a possible follow up mission simply known as the [Europa Lander](/wiki/Europa_Lander_(NASA) "Europa Lander (NASA)").[[57]](#cite_note-Flagship-57) Phase B continued into 2019.[[56]](#cite_note-Greicius-56) In addition, subsystem vendors were selected, as well as prototype hardware elements for the science instruments. Spacecraft sub-assemblies were built and tested as well.[[56]](#cite_note-Greicius-56)

### Fabrication and assembly

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Europa_Clipper.jpg/250px-Europa_Clipper.jpg)

On August 19, 2019, the Europa Clipper proceeded to Phase C: final design and fabrication.[[58]](#cite_note-NASA-20190819-58)

On March 3, 2022, the spacecraft moved on to Phase D: assembly, testing, and launch.[[59]](#cite_note-NASA-20220303-59) On June 7, 2022, the main body of the spacecraft was completed.[[60]](#cite_note-NASA-20220607-60) By August 2022, the high-gain antenna had completed its major testing campaigns.[[61]](#cite_note-Clipper_HGA_Tests-61)

By January 30, 2024, all of the science instruments were added to the spacecraft. The reason the instrument's electronics were aboard the spacecraft is because, while its antennas were added to the spacecraft's solar arrays at Kennedy Space Center later in the year, the former were not.[[62]](#cite_note-NASA-20240130-62) In March 2024, it was reported that the spacecraft underwent successful testing and was on track for launch later in the year.[[63]](#cite_note-63) In May 2024, the spacecraft arrived at Kennedy Space Center for final launch preparations.[[64]](#cite_note-64) In September 2024, final pre-launch review was successfully completed, clearing the way for launch.[[65]](#cite_note-65) In early October 2024, due to the incoming Hurricane Milton, the spacecraft was placed in secure storage for safekeeping until the hurricane passed.[[66]](#cite_note-auto-66)

### Launch

In July 2024, the spacecraft faced concerns of delay and missing the launch window because of a discovery in June 2024 that its components were not as radiation-hardened as previously believed.[[67]](#cite_note-67) However, over the summer, intensive re-testing of the transistor components in question found that they would likely be annealed enough to 'self-heal'.[[68]](#cite_note-NYT_transistor_healing-68)[[69]](#cite_note-CBS_transistor_healing-69) In September 2024, Europa Clipper was approved for a launch window opening on October 10, 2024;[[68]](#cite_note-NYT_transistor_healing-68)[[70]](#cite_note-70)[[69]](#cite_note-CBS_transistor_healing-69) however, on October 6, 2024, NASA announced that it would be standing down from the October 10 launch due to [Hurricane Milton](/wiki/Hurricane_Milton "Hurricane Milton"). Europa Clipper was finally launched on October 14, 2024.[[66]](#cite_note-auto-66)

### End of mission planning

The probe is scheduled to be crashed into Jupiter, [Ganymede](/wiki/Ganymede_(moon) "Ganymede (moon)"), or Callisto, to prevent it from crashing into Europa. In June 2022, lead project scientist Robert Pappalardo revealed that mission planners for Europa Clipper were considering disposing of the probe by crashing it into the surface of Ganymede in case an extended mission was not approved early in the main science phase. He noted that an impact would help the ESA's [JUICE](/wiki/Jupiter_Icy_Moons_Explorer "Jupiter Icy Moons Explorer") mission collect more information about Ganymede's surface chemistry.[[71]](#cite_note-LPI_OPAG_2022-71)[[72]](#cite_note-Waldek_2022-72) In a 2024 paper, Pappalardo said the mission would last four years in Jupiter orbit, and that the disposal was targeted for September 3, 2034, if NASA did not approve a mission extension.[[73]](#cite_note-Pappalardo_et_al_2024-73)

## Objectives

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/Photo_composite_of_suspected_water_plumes_on_Europa.jpg/250px-Photo_composite_of_suspected_water_plumes_on_Europa.jpg)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/EuropaClipper.jpg/250px-EuropaClipper.jpg)

The goals of Europa Clipper are to explore Europa, investigate its [habitability](/wiki/Habitability_of_natural_satellites "Habitability of natural satellites") and aid in the selection of a landing site for the proposed [Europa Lander](/wiki/Europa_Lander_(NASA) "Europa Lander (NASA)").[[52]](#cite_note-Lander_funding-52)[[74]](#cite_note-Europa_Lander_2013-74) This exploration is focused on understanding the three main requirements for life: [liquid water](/wiki/Water "Water"), [chemistry](/wiki/Chemistry "Chemistry"), and energy.[[75]](#cite_note-ASC_2017-75) Specifically, the objectives are to study:[[32]](#cite_note-OPAG_Update_2013-32)

The spacecraft carries [scientific instruments](#Scientific_equipment) which will be used to analyze the potential presence of geothermal activity and the moon's [induced magnetic field](/wiki/Europa_(moon)#Internal_structure "Europa (moon)"); which in turn will provide an indication to the presence of saline rich subsurface ocean(s).[[76]](#cite_note-:2-76)[[77]](#cite_note-:3-77)

### Strategy

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Europa_Clipper_Radiation.png/330px-Europa_Clipper_Radiation.png)

Because Europa lies well within the [harsh radiation fields](/wiki/Magnetosphere_of_Jupiter "Magnetosphere of Jupiter") surrounding Jupiter, even a radiation-hardened spacecraft in near orbit would be functional for just a few months.[[29]](#cite_note-Kane_2014-29) Most instruments can gather data far faster than the communications system can transmit it to Earth due to the limited number of antennas available on Earth to receive the scientific data.[[29]](#cite_note-Kane_2014-29) Therefore, another key limiting factor on science for a Europa orbiter is the time available to return data to Earth. In contrast, the amount of time during which the instruments can make close-up observations is less important.[[29]](#cite_note-Kane_2014-29)

Studies by scientists from the Jet Propulsion Laboratory show that by performing several flybys with many months to return data, the Europa Clipper concept will enable a US$2 billion mission to conduct the most crucial measurements of the canceled US$4.3 billion [Jupiter Europa Orbiter](/wiki/Jupiter_Europa_Orbiter "Jupiter Europa Orbiter") concept.[[29]](#cite_note-Kane_2014-29) Between each of the flybys, the spacecraft will have seven to ten days to transmit data stored during each brief encounter. That will let the spacecraft have up to a year of time to transmit its data compared to just 30 days for an orbiter. The result will be almost three times as much data returned to Earth, while reducing exposure to radiation.[[29]](#cite_note-Kane_2014-29) Europa Clipper will not orbit Europa, but will instead orbit [Jupiter](/wiki/Jupiter "Jupiter") and conduct 44 [flybys](/wiki/List_of_planetary_flybys "List of planetary flybys") of Europa, each at altitudes ranging from 25 to 2,700 km (16 to 1,678 mi) during its 3.5-year mission.[[4]](#cite_note-Gov_Home_About-4)[[2]](#cite_note-Phillips_2014-2)[[78]](#cite_note-78) A key feature of the mission concept is that Europa Clipper would use [gravity assists](/wiki/Gravity_assist "Gravity assist") from Europa, Ganymede and Callisto to change its trajectory, allowing the spacecraft to return to a different close approach point with each flyby.[[2]](#cite_note-Phillips_2014-2) Each flyby would cover a different sector of Europa to achieve a medium-quality global topographic survey, including ice thickness.[[79]](#cite_note-FPE-79) Europa Clipper could conceivably fly by at low altitude through the plumes of water vapor erupting from the moon's ice crust, thus sampling its subsurface ocean without having to land on the surface and drill through the ice.[[34]](#cite_note-March_2014-34)[[35]](#cite_note-Clark_2014-35)

The spacecraft is expected to receive a total ionizing dose of 2.8 megarads (28 kGy) during the mission. Shielding from Jupiter's harsh radiation belt will be provided by a radiation vault with 0.3 inches (7.6 mm) thick aluminum alloy walls, which enclose the spacecraft electronics.[[80]](#cite_note-80) To maximize the effectiveness of this shielding, the electronics are also nested in the core of the spacecraft for additional radiation protection.[[79]](#cite_note-FPE-79)

## Design and construction

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Europa-Clipper-Diagram.png/500px-Europa-Clipper-Diagram.png)

This mission is a flight of NASA's [Planetary Science Division](/wiki/Planetary_Science_Division "Planetary Science Division"), designated a [Large Strategic Science Mission](/wiki/Large_strategic_science_missions "Large strategic science missions"), and funded under the [Planetary Missions Program Office](/wiki/Planetary_Missions_Program_Office "Planetary Missions Program Office")'s [Solar System Exploration program](/wiki/Planetary_Missions_Program_Office "Planetary Missions Program Office") as its second flight.[[57]](#cite_note-Flagship-57)[[81]](#cite_note-sse-81) It is also supported by the new [Ocean Worlds Exploration Program](/wiki/Ocean_Worlds_Exploration_Program "Ocean Worlds Exploration Program").[[55]](#cite_note-OWEP-55)

The [spacecraft bus](/wiki/Spacecraft_bus "Spacecraft bus") is a 5-meter-long combination of a 150-cm-wide aluminum cylindrical propulsion module and a rectangular box.[[6]](#cite_note-APLpropulsionmodule-6) The electronic components are protected from the intense radiation by a 150-kilogram [titanium](/wiki/Titanium "Titanium"), [zinc](/wiki/Zinc "Zinc") and aluminum shielded vault in the box.[[4]](#cite_note-Gov_Home_About-4)[[79]](#cite_note-FPE-79)

### Power

Both [radioisotope thermoelectric generator](/wiki/Radioisotope_thermoelectric_generator "Radioisotope thermoelectric generator") (RTG) and [photovoltaic](/wiki/Photovoltaics "Photovoltaics") power sources were assessed to power the orbiter.[[82]](#cite_note-Eremenko2014-82) Although solar power is only 4% as intense at Jupiter as it is in Earth's orbit, powering a Jupiter orbital spacecraft by [solar panels](/wiki/Solar_panels_on_spacecraft "Solar panels on spacecraft") was demonstrated by the [Juno mission](/wiki/Juno_(spacecraft) "Juno (spacecraft)"). The alternative to solar panels was a [multi-mission radioisotope thermoelectric generator](/wiki/Multi-mission_radioisotope_thermoelectric_generator "Multi-mission radioisotope thermoelectric generator") (MMRTG), fueled with [plutonium-238](/wiki/Plutonium-238 "Plutonium-238").[[2]](#cite_note-Phillips_2014-2)[[79]](#cite_note-FPE-79) The power source has already been demonstrated in the [Mars Science Laboratory](/wiki/Mars_Science_Laboratory "Mars Science Laboratory") (MSL) mission. Five units were available, with one reserved for the [Mars 2020 rover mission](/wiki/Mars_2020_rover_mission "Mars 2020 rover mission") and another as backup. In September 2013, it was decided that the solar array was the less expensive option to power the spacecraft, and on October 3, 2014, it was announced that solar panels were chosen to power Europa Clipper. The mission's designers determined that solar power was both cheaper than plutonium and practical to use on the spacecraft.[[82]](#cite_note-Eremenko2014-82) Despite the increased weight of solar panels compared to plutonium-powered generators, the vehicle's mass had been projected to still be within acceptable launch limits.[[83]](#cite_note-83)

Each panel has a surface area of 18 m2 (190 sq ft) and produces 150 watts continuously when pointed towards the Sun while orbiting Jupiter.[[84]](#cite_note-Solar_panels-84) While in Europa's shadow, batteries will enable the spacecraft to continue gathering data. However, [ionizing radiation](/wiki/Ionizing_radiation "Ionizing radiation") can damage solar panels. The Europa Clipper's orbit will pass through Jupiter's intense magnetosphere, which is expected to gradually degrade the solar panels as the mission progresses.[[79]](#cite_note-FPE-79) The solar panels were provided by [Airbus Defence and Space](/wiki/Airbus_Defence_and_Space "Airbus Defence and Space"), Netherlands.[[85]](#cite_note-SextantMarch2018-Highlights-85)

### Propulsion

The propulsion subsystem was built by NASA's [Goddard Space Flight Center](/wiki/Goddard_Space_Flight_Center "Goddard Space Flight Center") in [Greenbelt, Maryland](/wiki/Greenbelt,_Maryland "Greenbelt, Maryland"). It is part of the Propulsion Module,[[86]](#cite_note-Rehm_2022-86) delivered by [Johns Hopkins Applied Physics Laboratory](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_Applied_Physics_Laboratory "Johns Hopkins Applied Physics Laboratory") in [Laurel, Maryland](/wiki/Laurel,_Maryland "Laurel, Maryland"). It is 3 metres (10 ft) tall, 1.5 metres (5 ft) in diameter and comprises about two-thirds of the spacecraft's main body. The propulsion subsystem carries nearly 2,700 kilograms (6,000 lb) of [monomethyl hydrazine](/wiki/Monomethyl_hydrazine "Monomethyl hydrazine") and [dinitrogen tetroxide](/wiki/Dinitrogen_tetroxide "Dinitrogen tetroxide") propellant, 50% to 60% of which will be used for the 6 to 8-hour Jupiter orbit insertion burn. The spacecraft has a total of 24 rocket engines rated at 27.5 N (6.2 lbf) thrust for attitude control and propulsion.[[6]](#cite_note-APLpropulsionmodule-6)

# Europa Clipper, a mission to Jupiter's icy moon

## Highlights

## Why send a spacecraft to Europa?

Beneath a thick shell of ice, Jupiter's moon Europa hosts a massive ocean that may be able to host life. Its salty ocean could have everything needed for a [thriving ecosystem](https://www.planetary.org/articles/the-making-of-life): energy, liquid water, and all the basic chemical ingredients for life as we know it. To find out more, NASA is sending the Europa Clipper mission to perform several close flybys of the Moon and investigate it in detail. This will be the first dedicated mission to explore a [world with a global ocean](https://www.planetary.org/articles/where-are-the-ocean-worlds-in-our-solar-system) besides Earth. By exploring Europaâs potential to host life, Europa Clipper will help scientists better understand the possibility of life on other worlds with subsurface oceans, including even [those outside](https://www.planetary.org/worlds/exoplanets) of the Solar System.

![Juno Europa flyby composite](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns=%27http://www.w3.org/2000/svg%27%20width=%271200%27%20height=%271057%27%20style=%27background:%23CCC%27%20/%3E)

Europaâs salty ocean is estimated to contain more than [twice the amount of water](https://www.planetary.org/space-images/water-on-europa-vs-water-on-europa)on Earth.  But all of that water could be locked under the moonâs icy shell, which may stretch anywhere from just 3 kilometers (about 2 miles) wide to over 30 kilometers (19 miles).  Though no sunlight could reach through that shell to power life, vents that release heat from the moon's interior could exist on its ocean floor. [Similar vents](https://www.planetary.org/space-images/hydrothermal-vents-on-ocean) at the bottom of Earth's oceans teem with life. Some scientists even think these vents could have been where life originally began on our planet.

Europaâs ocean might get fed from both above and below. At the ocean floor, Europaâs rocky interior could slowly churn and expose new material to any life present, while at the other side of the ocean, the moonâs icy shell could transport molecules on Europaâs surface down toward its ocean. Itâs also possible that Europa [launches plumes](https://www.planetary.org/articles/signs-of-life-enceladus) of water vapor out into space. It may even have lakes within its icy crust that could host life. A mission to Europa has long been one of the [scientific community's top priorities](https://www.planetary.org/space-policy/what-is-the-decadal-survey). Planetary Society members helped make the mission happen by sending tens of thousands of letters to their congressional representatives, sponsoring [events in Washington, D.C.](https://www.planetary.org/blogs/casey-dreier/2014/0718-capitol-hill-responds-to-the-lure-of-europa.html) to bring together scientists, politicians, and space advocates, and working with Congress to [help get the mission funded](https://www.planetary.org/press-room/releases/2013/1212-the-planetary-society-calls-for-a-mission-to-explore-europa.html).  NASA formally greenlit the mission in 2015.

![Europa Clipper gets its high-gain antenna](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns=%27http://www.w3.org/2000/svg%27%20width=%272400%27%20height=%271600%27%20style=%27background:%23CCC%27%20/%3E)

## How Europa Clipper works

NASA's Europa Clipper spacecraft lifted off [on a SpaceX Falcon Heavy rocket](https://www.nasa.gov/press-release/nasa-awards-launch-services-contract-for-europa-clipper-mission) from Kennedy Space Center in Florida on Oct. 14, 2024.  It will arrive at the Jupiter system in 2030, orbiting Jupiter instead of Europa to spend less time inside the planet's intense radiation field. The spacecraft will periodically dive through Jupiter's radiation, fly by Europa to collect data, and then move away again. Even with this orbit, the Europa Clipper instruments have to be housed in a radiation vault on the spacecraft to protect them from damage. But the strategy will allow Europa Clipper to study its target for years rather than days or months, with more than 40 flybys of Europa scheduled over 3.5 years.

## The Europa Clipper spacecraft

Including its solar arrays, Europa Clipper spans 30.5 meters (100 feet), longer than a basketball court. The main body of the spacecraft is packed with a wide range of [science instruments](https://europa.nasa.gov/mission/science-instruments/) to scan Europa from above and directly sample the moon's tenuous atmosphere.

![Europa Clipper on Basketball Court](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns=%27http://www.w3.org/2000/svg%27%20width=%27768%27%20height=%27482%27%20style=%27background:%23CCC%27%20/%3E)

An ice-penetrating radar instrument will map Europa's ice and the possible lakes within, while the Europa Clipper  Magnetometer (ECM) and Plasma Instrument for Magnetic Sounding (PIMS) will together measure the moonâs magnetic properties to provide strong evidence of the subsurface ocean. They will also help determine the depth of Europaâs icy shell and ocean. Two sets of cameras operating at different wavelengths â the Europa Thermal Emission Imaging System (E-THEMIS) and the Europa Imaging System (EIS) â will map the moonâs surface and search for plumes. The SUrface Dust Analyser (SUDA) will look for small particles ejected from Europa that could trace potential plumes. And finally, three spectrometers called the Europa Ultraviolet Spectrograph (Europa-UVS), the Mapping Imaging Spectrometer for Europa (MISE), and the Mass Spectrometer for Planetary Exploration (MASPEX) will measure the composition of Europaâs surface and atmosphere to get a sense of the makeup of its hidden ocean.

In addition to these Europa Clipper instruments, the spacecraft will host a propulsion module and a high-gain antenna. The radio antennas are meant to communicate with Earth, but they will also help serve the missionâs science goals. Slight changes in radio transmissions caused by [the Doppler Effect](https://www.planetary.org/articles/how-to-search-for-exoplanets) will allow scientists back on Earth to measure the spacecraftâs motion and in turn learn something about the structure and gravity of Europa.

On the side, Europa Clipper will scout locations where future space missions might land. [One mission under consideration](https://www.planetary.org/blogs/jason-davis/2017/nasas-audacious-europa.html) by NASA would spend a month on Europa's surface, feeding samples into instruments designed to detect organic materials.

## ADS

## The Europa Clipper Mission and its Final Stretch to Launch

#### Abstract

Scheduled to launch in October 2024, NASA's Europa Clipper will set out on a journey to explore the habitability of Jupiter's icy ocean world Europa. After a 5.5 yr cruise that includes gravity assists at Mars and Earth, the spacecraft will enter orbit around Jupiter and will perform nearly 50 flybys of Europa over a four-year period. To explore Europa as an integrated system and achieve a complete picture of its habitability, the Europa Clipper mission has three main science objectives to characterize: (1) the ice shell and ocean including their heterogeneity, properties, and surface-ice-ocean exchange; (2) Europa's composition including any non-ice materials on the surface and in the atmosphere, and any carbon-containing compounds; and (3) Europa's geology including surface features and localities of high science interest. Additionally, several cross-cutting science topics will be investigated through searching for any current or recent activity in the form of thermal anomalies and plumes, performing geodetic and radiation measurements, and assessing high-resolution, co-located observations at select sites to provide reconnaissance for a potential future landed mission. These science objectives will be accomplished using a highly capable suite of remote-sensing and in-situ instruments. The remote sensing payload consists of the Europa Ultraviolet Spectrograph (Europa-UVS), the Europa Imaging System (EIS) consisting of a wide and a narrow angle camera (WAC, NAC), the Mapping Imaging Spectrometer for Europa (MISE), the Europa Thermal Imaging System (E-THEMIS), and the Radar for Europa Assessment and Sounding: Ocean to Near-surface (REASON). The in-situ instruments are the Europa Clipper Magnetometer (ECM), the Plasma Instrument for Magnetic Sounding (PIMS), the SUrface Dust Analyzer (SUDA), and the MAss Spectrometer for Planetary Exploration (MASPEX). Gravity and radio science will be obtained using the spacecraft's telecommunication system, and valuable scientific data will be acquired by the spacecraft's radiation monitoring system.Assembly, test, and launch operations (ATLO) of the Europa Clipper spacecraft are progressing well, and the flight system integration and environmental testing has been completed at the Jet Propulsion Laboratory. Currently, the flight system is undergoing operations testing, and in May 2024, the spacecraft will be shipped to NASA's Kennedy Space Center at Cape Canaveral, Florida. There, the remaining integration activities will occur for the solar array and REASON antennas followed by final flight system tests. The launch period begins on 10 October 2024. To provide details on the mission's instruments and planned investigations, the Europa Clipper science team is publishing manuscripts in a special issue of Space Science Reviews, and the team continues to work towards optimizing science return through preparation of the mission's Strategic Science Planning Guide. As well, collaborative science opportunities with ESA's JUpiter ICy moons Explorer (JUICE) mission, which will overlap in its tour period at Jupiter and make observations of Europa, are being discussed informally among the science teams. Onward to Europa!
